speaker: interviewer
text: --long was it between the time your father started preparing you for war and actual war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Matter of weeks. It-- things happened very quickly, as history divulges. On September 1, 1939, the Germans attacked Poland, and we were at war. And the conversations that I'm recalling must have occurred within a week of that or two weeks at the most of that occurrence. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From that point forward, I knew, of course, that war was not quite what I thought it was because of the airplanes and the bombing and the ack ack and the, the, the machine gun fire that, that I would hear. But by then we had already moved, and that's a different part of the story. My, my life changed considerably the week before war began because my father, in preparation of the possibility of bombing, decided that we should move to his building-- his being what he had inherited from his parents-- which is a brick building near the outskirts of Bialystok. And where we lived was in the center of Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that his reasoning was that there was less chance of bombing in the outskirts of Bialystok in the first place. And the second place being that it was a brick building, two-story affair, it would be stronger and could withstand perhaps the shelling or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he took it upon himself to move the family. So we, we joined my father's mother-- my grandmother-- and Bobble, the aunt that lived there. And my father, mother, and I and my maternal grandmother all moved into the same premises-- the building on [POLISH] near the outskirts of Bialystok. And we did that prior to the war breaking out. That changed my life forever and things were never, from then on again, the same because events just carried away. But in preparation for that, my father also painted black all the windows because of the need for blackout, which the government requested that everyone do. So things were quite different once we, once we moved. And I heard the bombings, and I heard the gunfire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the fright came not from inside but from as a reaction to the fright that I saw in my mother's face and my father's. I myself didn't know, I don't think, the fright involved. It was a transmitted fright and, indeed, that transmitted itself very well. I became frightened as a consequence of that transmittal. I didn't know what to be frightened of until when I was older. But I knew that it was good enough for me that my parents were frightened. That frightened me. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they behave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was all very strange and very, I guess the word is frightening, because the, the tension was 24 hours. The schools were let out so that suddenly my parents were there all the time, at least in the first few days of the war. Pretty soon my father left altogether before the Germans, in fact, invaded Bialystok or entered Bialystok. He was already gone. But in the first few days we were all together. When-- the, the memory that I have is of one air raid where we would all huddle in the, in the vestibule of the building. My father didn't-- there was a basement, but he was afraid to put us in the basement in case the-- if building was hit we wouldn't be able to get out of the basement. So he would have us huddle near the door of the vestibule in the brick building so that we could quickly run through the door if anything hit the building. And I do recall-- I have a vivid memory of us huddling in, in, in silence as the bombs fell around us. And it was at that moment that I experienced for the very first time my own fright because, at that point, I understood that if something hits this building, no good would come of this. And I-- it was the first sort of awakening that war was a dangerous thing for me personally.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that Bobble and your bubbie, especially your [DRINKS] bubbie, were your security blankets. How did they react to the move and the change in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you could read it on their face. This was, this was a, a horror for them. There is no way you can describe the change in a person's face and demeanor. That normalcy had left forever. There was no longer a concern about what you eat or what you don't eat, what I leave on, on the plate that I didn't eat. The concern now for-- was for physical safety and that the family should hang together, and we shouldn't be apart or be caught in the streets alone or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a great deal of concern, and everybody tried to stay together. And as I said, until my father left, we were always together inside the house and mostly inside the house. Nobody wanted anybody to leave. Any of the bombing or the strafing, was that near enough for you to see or did you just hear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could just hear it. It wasn't until later that I saw bombed-out buildings. Ours didn't get hit but-- and my father was right that the edge of the city was not strafed or bombed, and, and it was all more or less pointed for inside the city. And of course Poland fell in seven days. There wasn't much need for a lot of bombing. But I didn't see any of it myself until after the fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he go away? Where did he go away? And why did he go away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as a member of the city council, the mayor and the city council had met. And in their discussion right directly as after war broke out, probably the very first day, they decided-- or it was the decision of the council-- that the entire council as well as all other prominent city folk-- mens folk-- should leave because it was rumored, and I learned later correctly rumored, that the Germans, once they entered the city, would use the city councilmen and other prominent city officials or people as hostages should anything go wrong in the city. In other words, if some order of the German army was violated, then they would be held accountable and be shot. 10 of them would be shot, or five of them would be shot in retribution. And this was a accepted principle that they believed-- they, the city council and the mayor-- believed would happen. And so they decided that if they left the city, they couldn't be arrested for this hostage purpose. And so it was agreed that they would rent the vans and on a given night-- actually, in the middle of the night-- they left in a van for parts unknown. I never knew, my mother never knew, where they left. But they were obviously fleeing from Poland, which was invaded by the Germans. So it was an idea to get away from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father kiss you good night--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --before he left or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. [DRINKS] The way it went-- the way it went is that my, my mother woke me in the middle of that night to tell me and dress me-- it was like 2:00 AM-- that we were going to say goodbye to my father. Until then I didn't know he was leaving. So they kept that from me. And I dressed in the middle of the night, and it was that night that stands out most in my mind because as we walked through the deserted Bialystok streets, I could hear the machine gun fire and the airplanes above. And it was a war scene, and it stands out in my memory because the gunfire echoed through the buildings as we walked. And you couldn't tell where it was coming from because there was a constant echoing going on as if it was coming from every direction. That was a most frightening, probably the most frightening, night of my life 'til then.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a little over--

speaker: interviewer
text: Seven and a half.

speaker: interviewee
text: --seven. Yes. And we arrived at a, at a designated place where there was this big truck. I, I could only see the outlines of it. This is pitch black, and it's in the middle of the night and a lot of people milling around and everybody hugging and saying goodbye. It was then that my father said goodbye. My, my mother had advised me and told me that my father was leaving and the reason for that. And I said goodbye to my father. And it was a very quick-- in my memory-- a very quick goodbye that didn't last very long. My father wasn't a hugger anyway. He was not the kind that would spend a lot of time at hugging someone. It was more kind of thing that you just did. I wasn't-- he didn't like to show a lot of emotion and so--

speaker: interviewee
text: The love was in the preparation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The moving, the blacking--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the love was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the windows.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was accepted that it was there. It was implicit, but you didn't have to exhibit it. And there was a hug also between him and my mother. My mother was, of course, in tears and that, of course, was a reaction I felt very strongly, the fact that my mother was crying. But there were a lot of people crying. You could hear the tears.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a frightening moment. There was no question that that left an indelible memory in my mind that I remember to this day. We said our goodbyes. We left before the truck left because that's how they wanted it. And so we turned around along with many people, and everyone dispersed. And we returned at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember whether the whole thing took more than two hours, the trip there and back. Maybe less. And we returned and, and, and of course, I eventually went to sleep. But it left, as I said, an indelible memory of that evening and that night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] war continued for another three, four days. I was in a barbershop getting-- about to get a haircut, sitting on a high stool, when-- my mother was with me. And suddenly we heard someone shouting from outside on the street. [SPEAKING POLISH] The meaning, of course, is is let down the grates. The guest has arrived. The guest being the Germans. I didn't interpret it that way. I didn't quite know what that meant. My mother knew what that meant, and the barber knew what that meant. And they very quickly got me off the stool. My mother put my coat back on. It was September, and there was a chill in the air so she dressed me. And we rushed out. And another memory that I have that has plagued me to this day is the scene outside where everybody was running. The word spread like wildfire that the enemy had arrived, and everybody was running, obviously, home, as we ran. And my mother and I literally ran home from there to, to our residence outside of-- just outside of Bialystok's inner city. It wasn't long after that, maybe three, four hours later, that because of our location near the outskirts of Bialystok that we were the first to see the Germans arrive. It was a strange, eerie, frightening feeling. I had made a, a private peek hole so that I could look out the blackened windows that my father had painted for the air raid so that I could watch. And I was watching from an upstairs window. And the strangest thing was that five German soldiers on horseback showed up first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and slowly came in riding to the city. I was mesmerized by the scene. They were dressed, of course, in uniform. What followed were huge tanks, what looked like mammoth vehicles, at least to me. And there were hundreds and hundreds, and they rumbled. That was the thing you heard first, the rumbling. So you knew something was coming. And then later these tanks came and, of course, it was an overwhelming feeling. There was no longer any shooting or anything. These were just tanks coming through. And they were followed-- what seemed like hundreds of tanks. It took a long time. And they were followed by trucks, hundreds and hundreds of trucks. And on these trucks, inside them-- some of the trucks were covered wagons. Some of them were open-- German soldiers all in helmets and with guns. And of course, you've seen this scene, I'm sure, in movies as the, as the, as the, the enemy soldiers came into a foreign city like Bialystok was. That was the-- the rumbling of the tanks and the, the vehicles that followed is something you don't forget because, first of all, it lasted a long time. I don't know if it didn't last many hours into the night. Eventually I stopped watching. But it was a frightening, frightening, feeling. I knew war wasn't sword fighting on the big streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this point, my mother-- after a few days, there were proclamations, and you couldn't-- 6 o'clock curfew. You couldn't congregate in the streets. Everybody had to register. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there was all kinds of proclamations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were also rumors that began to infiltrate the Jewish community about special atrocities, not ghetto or anything like that, not anything near extermination that eventually came about. These were the kind of atrocities where a particular individual, usually a Jew, was accosted by soldiers, beaten up, or ridiculed in one fashion or another. And there were many stories about that kind of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the incident I saw wasn't someone getting beat up. I saw something far worse. We lived, as I said, on the outskirts of Bialystok, and it overlooked a cemetery. The-- it was a Jewish cemetery, actually. The cemetery, however, was a convenient shortcut into town that was used during the day quite often by people that lived in the surrounding area, not necessarily Jews. Everyone used the cemetery as a quick way to get into town. But curfew was at 6:00 PM. And one evening as I was looking through my peephole upstairs in a window, I saw a young girl-- she had to be in her teens, maybe 17, maybe 18, maybe 16-- leave for the, the shortcut. And I realized that it was near curfew, and she was rushing. And she had her head down as if that way she won't be detected. And I saw two soldiers-- German soldiers-- see her go into the cemetery, and they took after her. They caught her. I, of course, didn't know at the time that it was a rape. I didn't know what rape meant until years later, but that's what it was. And that's what I witnessed. I witnessed her scream and the soldiers jump on her, hold her down, and so forth. And that memory, of course, haunted me [SIGHS] in later years. Of course, I had heard the girl scream, and so I knew it was violent. I didn't quite know exactly what it was, but I knew it wasn't pleasant for her. And the scream and her fear transmitted to me, and I'm, as I say, was haunted for that for many years. I would remember it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she get up?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in nightmares. I never saw that. It was near nightfall, and I never saw what happened afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know if she was killed or not.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think she was killed because there was nothing in the morning to indicate that. So I really think it was just the rape and nothing more, as if you can say nothing more, but that's what it was. So that is something that I did experience on a personal level watching, but I didn't see any other physical [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] injuries. The, the stories did filter down to me because you couldn't help but overhear various stories. Eventually my mother decided that we should move back to our own house because she was afraid that standing empty as it was, it was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] target of looting perhaps and that it was best that we occupied the premises. So we moved back. Bombing had, after all, stopped, and there was no reason to remain in my father's building.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved back, and within a day after our moving back, German soldiers arrived to look for my father just as they had expected would happen. They wanted him. They wanted to arrest him as a hostage.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came and searched the premises, poked in the closets and the drawers, talked very loud in a language I recognized was-- had many similarities to Yiddish, which of course I knew. But it was in a harsh tongue, and I didn't fully understand what it is they were saying. My mother responded. But the fear and the tears welling in her eyes transmitted to me and my grandmother with us. And it was the personal-- that brought the war very much personally to me. It was the first time that the soldiers were that close to me. In fact, my memory is that one of them tapped me on the head. I could only-- you know, I didn't-- I never looked really up at them, but I, I looked at their boots. And it was their boots that left the memory and the imprint on me. Well, my father wasn't there so they couldn't get him, and they left eventually. But it was the first real turning point which proved the, the difference between life and death perhaps in our lives. Clearly had he been there, he would have been arrested, and this story would have been totally different. So here was a turning point in our lives, the fact he had escaped, left, and never was there to be arrested. It, it of course [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wasn't the first turning point in this story, but it's clearly a critical juncture. The Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then were making a pact with Stalin. Stalin and Hitler, the Russians and the Germans, made a pact, a non-aggression treaty. And the Russians bought time and Hitler, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for whatever political reasons, divided Poland with Stalin so that the part that was Bialystok would be turned over to the Russians, and the rest of Poland would be for the Germans. That meant that on a given day the Germans would march out, and the Russians would march in. And it was a, a real festive kind of feeling because the, the real feared enemy was the Germans, much more so than the Russians, or at least it was felt that way. And the day that the Germans were marching out of the city was a-- somewhat of a celebratory day. My mother took me to watch the scene where, in the big street-- middle of town, which was a divided thoroughfare with flowers in the middle and traffic going in one direction and the other side traffic going in the opposite direction, and in fact, the Germans marched out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The, the, the throngs that came to witness the scene-- maybe it was half of Bialystok was there to witness the scene-- was quiet as the Germans marched out. There was hardly a sound. But then, an hour or so later, the Russian army started marching in from the other direction. And it was a very happy occasion for the Bialystokers. They cheered the Russian army. Obviously, it was the lesser of two evils. And, and suddenly red flags showed up among some of the people. There were probably a number of communists around, too, that applauded it from a political point of view as well. But generally speaking, even the Jews were certainly less fearful of the Russians than they were of the Germans, with good reason, historic reasons. So there was a, a festive feeling among the Jewish population and the Polish population, and I watched as the Russian army marched in. Behind the Russian army came all of the people who had ran away, who had left Bialystok for fear of their lives. The councilmen, other prominent citizens, and others who had left Bialystok now felt the safety of the Russian presence to come back to Bialystok, all except my father and one other, other friend of his who did not return, much to the disappointment, of course, to my mother and myself who expected that they would return with all the others.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was, in fact, besides herself in worry that my father didn't return and perhaps something horrible had befallen him. What was his motivation of not returning? Where was he? The people that could tell her, the other friends that had returned, simply said that he was a very stubborn fool who wouldn't trust the Russians because he was a staunch anti-communist-- he had written extensively, as a Bundist, against communism-- and would not return with the Russian army, that they had argued with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told him that it would be much safer for them. But he refused to listen and said that he didn't trust the Russians any more than the Germans and would not return and didn't return with them. And he and this friend didn't come back. This was a blow to my mother, of course, and to me by transmittal of her feelings that my father was still off somewhere unbeknownst to anyone where. They had lived in Lithuania is what we were told by the others, sometimes in the forests, sometimes near Kovno, hiding most of the time, and that he was going to probably continue that existence but where, when, and what he was doing right then and there, nobody knew. Of course what happened is a [SIGHS] really another turning point in our lives, is that within 24 hours of the Russian return, all of the people who were city councilmen that had returned to Bialystok were arrested by the what is now the KGB-- it was the NKVD at that time-- and sent off to Siberia, some of them perishing along the way. And of course, again, my father had made the right decision. Had he returned, he was surely going to be arrested because the NKVD came to look for him to arrest him.
